question: What is the default order of statement execution in a Java method?
option_a: Random order depending on the JVM
option_b: Linear (sequential) — one statement after another
option_c: Reverse order from last to first
option_d: Parallel execution of all statements simultaneously
description: Statements run sequentially, top to bottom, unless a control statement (if, loop, etc.) changes the flow.

question: Which of the following is NOT one of the Java conditional statements?
option_b: if-else statement
option_d: switch statement
description: while is a loop (iteration), not a conditional. The conditionals are if, if-else, and switch.

question: What type of value must the condition in an if statement evaluate to?
option_b: A String value
option_d: A floating-point number
description: An if condition must be a boolean expression. Unlike C, an int cannot stand in for a condition.

question: In an if statement, what happens when the condition evaluates to false?
option_a: The statement inside the if block is executed twice
option_b: The program terminates with an error
option_c: The statement inside the if block is skipped
option_d: The condition is re-evaluated until it becomes true
description: When the condition is false, the if body is skipped and execution continues after it.

question: Which keyword is used to introduce a conditional check in Java?
option_b: check
option_d: test
description: The if keyword introduces a conditional check.

question: Which relational operator checks whether two values are equal in Java?
description: == tests equality; = is assignment. Java has no === operator.

question: What does the relational operator != mean?
option_b: Greater than
option_d: Exactly equal to
description: != means not equal to.

question: Which has higher precedence — arithmetic operators or relational operators?
option_b: They have identical precedence
option_c: Arithmetic operators have higher precedence than relational operators
option_d: Precedence depends on the variable types involved
description: Arithmetic binds tighter than relational, so a + 5 > b computes a + 5 first.

question: What does the logical AND operator (&&) return?
option_a: true if at least one operand is true
option_b: true only if both operands are true
option_c: true if both operands are false
option_d: Always returns false
description: && is true only when both operands are true.

question: What is the result of: true || false?
option_b: true
option_d: Compile error
description: Logical OR (||) is true if at least one operand is true → true.

question: What is the result of: !true?
option_d: false
description: Logical NOT (!) inverts the value, so !true is false.

question: In what order is this condition evaluated: total < MAX + 5 && !found?
option_b: + first, then <, then !, then &&
option_d: All operators evaluate simultaneously
description: Precedence order: arithmetic + → relational < → logical ! → logical &&.

question: What does 'short-circuit evaluation' mean for the && operator?
option_a: Both operands are always evaluated regardless of the left operand
option_b: If the left operand is false, the right operand is NOT evaluated
option_c: If the left operand is true, the right operand is NOT evaluated
option_d: The operator skips every third evaluation
description: For &&, if the left side is false the whole expression is false, so the right side is skipped.

question:
Why is this code safe due to short-circuit evaluation?
if (count != 0 && total/count > MAX)
option_a: Because division is always safe in Java
option_b: Because if count == 0, total/count is never evaluated, avoiding division by zero
option_c: Because the compiler automatically inserts a zero check
option_d: Because total/count always returns 0 when count is 0
description: If count == 0, the left side is false and total/count is never reached, preventing a divide-by-zero.

question: Which correctly describes precedence among logical operators in Java?
option_b: && has higher precedence than || and !
option_c: ! has higher precedence than &&, which is higher than ||
option_d: All logical operators have identical precedence
description: Order is ! > && > ||.

option_b: Only when sum <= MAX
option_d: Never, because it is inside the if block
description: Without braces, only the next single statement belongs to the if. The println is outside it, so it always runs.

question: In an if-else statement, how many of the two branches execute?
option_b: Neither executes if the condition is null
option_c: Exactly one — either statement1 or statement2, but not both
option_d: Both execute if the condition is true
description: An if-else runs exactly one branch depending on the condition.

option_d: A runtime exception
description: Since -3 is not >= 0, the else branch runs, printing Negative input.

option_a: Sets inventoryError to true whenever total equals stock + warehouse
option_b: Sets inventoryError to true whenever total does not equal stock + warehouse
option_d: Compares total to stock only, ignoring warehouse
description: != flags a mismatch: the error flag is set when total differs from stock + warehouse.

question: Which is the correct syntax for an if-else statement in Java?
option_b: if (condition) statement1; else statement2;
option_d: when (condition) do statement1; else statement2;
description: Java syntax: if (condition) statement1; else statement2; (parentheses around the condition).

option_b: The inner if (y > 0)
option_d: Neither — the else is a syntax error
description: The 'dangling else' binds to the nearest unmatched if — here the inner if (y > 0).

question: A program prints 'Positive' if n > 0, 'Negative' if n < 0, and 'Zero' if n = 0. Which structure is most appropriate?
option_b: Two separate if statements with no else
option_c: A nested if / else-if / else structure
option_d: A switch statement with cases > and <
description: Three mutually-exclusive outcomes are best handled with an if / else-if / else chain.

question: What is the purpose of the switch statement?
option_a: To repeat a block of code multiple times
option_b: To evaluate an expression and match it against several possible cases
option_d: To handle exceptions thrown at runtime
description: switch compares one expression against multiple constant case values.

question: In a switch statement, what does each case contain?
option_a: A range of values and a loop body
option_b: A boolean condition and two branches
option_c: A constant value and a list of statements
option_d: A variable name and its data type
description: Each case has a constant value followed by statements to run on a match.

question: What is the role of the break statement inside a switch case?
option_a: It causes the entire program to terminate
option_b: It transfers control to the end of the switch, preventing fall-through
option_c: It forces re-evaluation of the switch expression
option_d: It skips the next case and continues to the one after
description: break exits the switch so execution does not fall through into later cases.

question: What happens if a break statement is omitted from a case in a switch?
option_b: The switch terminates immediately
option_c: Execution falls through into the next case
option_d: The default case is executed instead
description: Without break, control falls through and the following case(s) also execute.

question: Which data type CANNOT be used as the switch expression (per the lecture)?
option_b: char
option_d: byte
description: switch works with integral types (byte, short, int, long), char, String, and enums — but not double/float.

question: Which data type CAN be used as the switch expression?
option_b: float
option_d: char
description: char is allowed; boolean and floating-point types are not.

question: What is the implicit comparison tested in a switch statement?
option_b: Equality (==) between the expression and each case value
option_d: Range membership
description: A switch tests equality between the expression and each constant case value.

question: What is the purpose of the default case in a switch statement?
option_a: It always executes before any other case
option_b: It executes if no other case value matches the switch expression
option_c: It resets the switch expression to zero
option_d: It is mandatory and causes a compile error if omitted
description: default is the fallback that runs when no case matches.

question: Is the default case mandatory in a switch statement?
option_a: Yes — omitting it causes a compile error
option_b: Yes — omitting it causes a runtime exception
option_c: No — it is optional; if absent and no case matches, control moves past the switch
option_d: No — but without it the switch always falls through to the last case
description: default is optional; with no match and no default, the switch simply does nothing.

question: Can a switch statement perform relational checks such as case (x > 5)?
option_a: Yes — any boolean expression is valid in a case
option_b: No — case values must be constants; relational checks are not supported
option_c: Yes — but only for integer types
option_d: Yes — using a special relational keyword
description: case labels must be constant values; use if/else-if for relational ranges.

question: Which correctly describes 'fall-through' behaviour in a switch?
option_a: The program crashes when no case matches
option_b: The default case executes before any matching case
option_c: Without a break, execution continues into the next case regardless of its value
option_d: The switch expression is re-evaluated for each case
description: Fall-through means missing breaks let execution spill into following cases.

question: For a month-number switch (1–12), how should you handle an invalid number such as 13?
option_b: Add a default: case that prints an error/invalid message
option_c: Add an else clause after the switch
option_d: No handling is needed; the switch ignores it
description: A default: case catches any value outside 1–12 and reports it as invalid.

option_d: No grade is assigned
description: Both independent ifs are true, so grade is set to 'A' then overwritten to 'B'. An else if chain would fix this.

question: To classify a number as positive, negative, or zero using if-else-if, how many condition checks are needed at minimum?
option_b: Two checks (e.g. > 0 and < 0; zero is the else)
option_d: Four checks
description: Two tests (> 0 and < 0) suffice; the remaining case (zero) is handled by the final else.

question: In a vowel/consonant switch on a char, which approach handles case-insensitivity correctly?
option_a: Add only uppercase cases: case 'A': case 'E': ...
option_b: Convert the character to uppercase (or lowercase) before the switch, then check one set of cases
option_c: Use a separate switch for uppercase and another for lowercase
option_d: Use relational operators inside each case
description: Normalise the char with Character.toUpperCase() first, then test a single set of cases.

option_b: Two Three
option_d: Nothing — no default
description: Matching case 2 with no breaks falls through case 3 as well → Two Three.

question: Which logical expression is equivalent to !(a && b)?
option_d: a && b
description: By De Morgan's law, !(a && b) equals !a || !b.

question: A program assigns grades A (>=90), B (>=80), C (>=70), D (>=60), F otherwise. What is the minimum number of else-if branches (excluding the final else)?
description: One if plus 4 else if branches cover A–D, with the final else giving F.

question: Which correctly rewrites the nested if into an if / else-if / else chain for positive (>0), zero (==0), negative (<0)?
option_b: if(n>0) p(); else if(n==0) z(); else ng();
option_c: if(n>0) p(); else z(); else if(n<0) ng();
option_d: switch(n) { case >0: p(); case 0: z(); default: ng(); }
description: An if / else if / else chain is the correct mutually-exclusive structure.

question: Is the if keyword a Java reserved word?
option_b: No — it can be used as a variable name
option_d: Only in older Java versions
description: if is a reserved keyword and cannot be used as an identifier.

question: What is the equality operator in Java?
option_b: == (double equals)
option_d: equals only
description: == tests equality; a single = assigns a value.

question: When does the logical OR (||) return false?
option_a: When at least one operand is true
option_b: Only when both operands are false
option_d: When the operands differ
description: || is false only when both operands are false.

question: Can a switch statement use a boolean as its expression type in Java?
option_b: No — switch needs an integral type, char, String, or enum; boolean is not allowed
option_d: Only in Java 8+
description: boolean is not a valid switch type; use an if/else for boolean logic.

question: In the expression total < MAX + 5, which operation is evaluated first?
option_b: The addition (+), because arithmetic has higher precedence than relational
option_c: They evaluate at the same time
option_d: Whichever appears leftmost
description: Arithmetic binds tighter, so MAX + 5 is computed before the < comparison.
